figures.gloss: The chosen one marked at birth — born with teeth, a caul, or an extra bone — taken by sickness and dream, who battles rival táltos in the shape of bull or stallion and reads the weather; recorded not in scripture but in 16th-18th-c. trial records and village belief collected into the 20th century.
figures.facet: A specialist who outlived his religion: the táltos persists as a folk-belief fossil under a thousand years of Christianity — whether he descends from steppe shamanism (Diószegi's thesis) or is European wizard-lore in Hungarian dress remains genuinely contested, and that dispute is this shard's live wound.
figures.provenance: Vilmos Diószegi, A sámánhit emlékei a magyar népi műveltségben (1958); Éva Pócs on táltos figures in witch-trial records; earlier synthesis in Ipolyi's Magyar mythologia (1854). All reconstruction — no direct pagan-era source describes the táltos.

figures.gloss: The tree reaching to heaven of Hungarian folk-tale, sun and moon in its branches and otherworlds in its crown, which only the marked herd-boy can climb — read by the reconstructors as the world-tree of the lost Magyar cosmology surviving as a tale-type.
figures.facet: An axis mundi preserved in the nursery: the cosmological tree survives not as doctrine but as a folk-tale scaffold, its climber a disguised táltos — the clearest case of the reconstruction's method, reading cosmology back out of entertainment.
figures.provenance: János Berze Nagy, Égigérő fa (1958); Diószegi 1958 on the tale-tree as world-tree. The cosmological reading is scholarly reconstruction from folklore, not an attested pagan doctrine.

figures.gloss: The raptor of Emese's dream: a great bird comes to the ancestress and from the dream the dynasty flows — Álmos is born and the Árpáds reckon their right to rule from it; the bird is named turul in Kézai's chronicle (~1282), the dream told earlier by Anonymus (~1200).
figures.facet: Dynastic legitimation in steppe idiom: heaven's warrant delivered through a bird to a foremother, not through anointing — one of the few genuinely medieval-attested fragments of the old religion, which is why the reconstruction leans so hard on it.
figures.provenance: Anonymus, Gesta Hungarorum (~1200, the Emese dream); Simon of Kéza, Gesta Hunnorum et Hungarorum (~1282, the name turul). Chronicle-attested; its pagan-era meaning is inference.

figures.gloss: The twin brothers who chase the csodaszarvas, the wondrous hind, into the Maeotis marshes; the deer vanishes, the land is good, they take wives there and father the Huns and the Magyars — the origin legend as Kézai's chronicle (~1282) tells it.
figures.facet: A migration remembered as a hunt: the guiding-animal origin legend is a widespread steppe pattern, and here it does double duty — founding the people and (in Kézai's hands) chaining the Magyars to the prestige of Attila's Huns, myth already working as politics.
figures.provenance: Simon of Kéza, Gesta Hunnorum et Hungarorum (~1282); the Hun link is medieval construction, the guiding-hind motif has steppe parallels noted throughout the scholarship.

figures.gloss: The Blessed Lady of childbed and of the guarded days — folk custom kept the 'bed of Boldogasszony' for new mothers — whose name the first Christian generations were, by tradition credited to bishop St. Gerard, permitted to keep as the Hungarian title of the Virgin Mary.
figures.facet: A goddess visible only as the shape she left in Mary: nothing of her survives except the seam of the syncretism itself — the pre-Christian mother reconstructed from the exact contours of what Christianity chose to absorb rather than erase.
figures.provenance: The Gerard attribution comes from medieval hagiographic tradition; the goddess-reading argued by Lajos Kálmány, Boldogasszony, ősvallásunk istenasszonya (1885); carried here as reconstruction, per the shard's whole register.

edges.note: Diószegi argued the táltos is inherited Siberian-steppe shamanism surviving in Hungary; the typological kinship (election by mark and sickness, animal-form spirit-battle, trance) is real, but genetic descent remains contested — tiered as analogy, not descent.

The tale-tree the marked boy climbs beside the notched pole the shaman ascends — Diószegi read them as one inheritance, but the seam is held at structural kinship: the same vertical cosmos, the Hungarian one surviving only as story.

Heaven's withdrawable warrant to rule, delivered in steppe idiom — the Turul-dream legitimates the Árpáds as the sky's kut legitimates a khan; the Magyars were a steppe polity, so influence is plausible, but no document ties the two, hence analogy.

The deep-Uralic argument: two ecstatic wonder-workers of related language families read as echoes of a shared shamanic substrate — suggestive, unprovable across four thousand years of separation, and both reconstructed; flagged speculative.

The near mother-power guarding childbed and infant — Boldogasszony's birth-bed customs beside Umai's cradle-watch; a structural kinship of office, with the Hungarian figure herself a reconstruction read out of Marian syncretism.
The climber and the tree: in the tales only the marked one can ascend the sky-high tree — the reconstruction's two central fragments interlock as technique and road.

dossier.essence: The reconstructed pre-Christian religion of the Magyars (Hungarian ősvallás), glimpsed around the conquest of the Carpathian Basin (late 9th century) and ended as open practice by Stephen I's Christianization (1000). No practicing community survived: the tradition exists as fragments — dynastic legends in the medieval chronicles, a world-tree preserved in folk tales, and the táltos of village belief and witch-trial records — assembled by scholars into the hypothesis of a lost Magyar shamanic religion, and taken up by modern neopagan revivalists.
dossier.cosmos: The reconstruction posits a layered cosmos joined by a vertical axis: the sky-high tree (az égig érő fa) of Hungarian folk tale, sun and moon in its branches and otherworlds in its crown, which only the marked one can climb. Heaven's favor descends in steppe idiom — the Turul bird of Emese's dream fathers the Árpád line's right to rule, and the wondrous hind leads Hunor and Magor to the land of their descendants. What the pagan Magyars taught of death is unrecorded; conquest-era graves with horses and goods imply a journeying dead, but doctrine is inference.
dossier.practice: No rite survives intact, and the evidence is indirect: Ibn Rusta's notice of the pagan Magyars (early 10th century), chronicle fragments, and above all the táltos — a specialist marked at birth by teeth, caul, or extra bone, called through sickness and dream, battling rival táltos in the shape of bull or stallion — documented in 16th–18th-century witch trials and village belief collected into the 20th century. Whether the táltos continues steppe shamanism (Diószegi's thesis) or is European wizard-lore in Hungarian dress remains genuinely contested. The modern ősvallás movement practices a reconstruction, not a continuity.
dossier.canon: No native scripture: the sources are the medieval chronicles — Anonymus's 'Gesta Hungarorum' (~1200) and Simon of Kéza's 'Gesta Hunnorum et Hungarorum' (~1282) — plus witch-trial records and the folklore collections from which Ipolyi (1854) and Diószegi (1958) built the reconstruction.
dossier.reflects: The religious impulse read backward: a sacred system recovered from what outlived it — nursery tales, trial records, dynastic legend — showing how cosmology persists as story long after its doctrine dies.
sources: Vilmos Diószegi, 'A sámánhit emlékei a magyar népi műveltségben' (1958)
Martyn Rady, 'The Gesta Hungarorum of Anonymus, the Anonymous Notary of King Béla: A Translation' (2009)
Éva Pócs, 'Between the Living and the Dead: A Perspective on Witches and Seers in the Early Modern Age' (1999)

links.cite: Vilmos Diószegi, 'A sámánhit emlékei a magyar népi műveltségben' (1958)
Martyn Rady, 'The Gesta Hungarorum of Anonymus, the Anonymous Notary of King Béla: A Translation' (2009)
